text: The video demonstrates the modular approach for total mesorectal excision using a robotic system. Ports are placed with specific distances, including a camera port at R-II and an assistant port between R-III and R-IV. In obese patients, an additional assistant port is used for optimized exposure. The small bowel is displaced medially, the transverse colon and greater omentum are positioned cephalad, and the pelvis is emptied using gauze. The robot is docked from the left side of the patient.

The surgeon begins dissection at the sacral promontory, using the robotic system to ligate the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and vein (IMV). The fat surrounding the IMA is dissected and skeletonized, with a vascular stapler applied one centimeter distal to its takeoff to protect the superior hypogastric nerve plexus. The IMV is identified near the duodenojejunal junction (DJ), isolated, clipped, and divided at the inferior border of the pancreas.

The surgeon performs medial to lateral mobilization, dissecting superiorly between the fascia gerota and mesocolon to avoid bleeding. The difference in tissue color aids in identifying the avascular plane. The surgeon's left hand provides traction while the assistant holds the fascia for counter traction. Dissection continues at the lower border of the pancreas, with a gauze piece placed over the pancreas to prevent injury, facilitating splenic flexure mobilization.

The surgeon continues lateral dissection, visibly opening the peritoneum. Inferiorly, the ureters are identified by their peristaltic movements, with careful attention to avoid injury to the ureter, iliac, and conodal vessels.

The surgeon visually identifies the inner coccygeal ligament during inferior dissection and cuts it to achieve complete mobilization of the rectum. A rectal exam is performed to assess the tumor, followed by division of the rectum using a stapler.

The surgeon extracts the specimen and performs an anastomosis, with the intact mesorectum visible. The left and right iliac vessels and pelvic nerves are observed, along with the smooth surface of the pelvis. An endosannin green test illuminates the left iliac vessels, showing clonic perfusion. Yellow and red arrows indicate perfused and non-perfused areas of the colon, aiding in determining the resection line. The anastomosis is completed using a circular stapler.
